items.prompt: A review note says only, "clear bit k with x AND complement(1 shifted left by k)," but does not state a word width. What is missing?

options.text: The width that limits complement and defines valid bit positions.
The numeric sort order of inputs.
A proof that x XOR x is zero.
The number of subsets represented.

feedback.reason: Complement flips every position in the declared word.
feedback.details: Without a width model, the high-bit pattern and portability of the expression are underspecified.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.xor_identity: The operation is clear, not XOR cancellation.

items.prompt: Two variables contain the same 8-bit pattern 11111111, but one is unsigned and one is signed two's complement. Which diagnosis is correct?

options.text: They have the same stored bits but numeric values 255 and -1.
Signedness cannot change numeric interpretation.
The stored patterns must differ.
All-ones is invalid in one of the types.

feedback.reason: Bit pattern and numeric interpretation are separate concepts.
feedback.details: Review must track both width and signedness.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.same_value: The sign bit changes the signed value.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.different_bits: The pattern is explicitly the same.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.both_invalid: It is valid in both interpretations.

items.prompt: A developer replaces signed integer division by 2 with arithmetic right shift for all values. Which counterexample exposes the assumption?

options.text: -3 when division truncates toward zero.

feedback.reason: Arithmetic right shift of -3 commonly yields -2 through sign extension.
feedback.details: Division by 2 truncating toward zero yields -1, so the substitution is not universally valid.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.positive_four: Both operations produce 2 under ordinary models.

constraints: Compare arithmetic right shift against signed division truncating toward zero.

items.prompt: A shift count may equal or exceed the fixed word width. What is the correct review conclusion?

options.text: The behavior must be specified for the target language or rejected by a portable precondition.
Every implementation guarantees zero.
Every implementation throws.
Every implementation reduces the count modulo width.

feedback.reason: Oversized shift semantics vary across languages and machines.
feedback.details: Portable content should constrain counts to 0 <= k < width unless a target-specific rule is declared.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.always_zero: Some mask the count or use other rules.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.same_as_modulo: That is common in some environments but not universal.

items.prompt: A proposed proof treats XOR as ordinary addition because both combine bits. What is the defect?

options.text: XOR adds each bit modulo two without carries, so it differs from general arithmetic addition.
XOR requires sorted operands.
Addition does not operate on bit representations.
XOR cannot be applied to integer words.

feedback.reason: For example, 1 XOR 1 is 0 while 1 + 1 is 2.
feedback.details: The no-carry property enables cancellation but prevents XOR from replacing arithmetic sum.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.addition_no_bits: Addition does, but with carry propagation.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.xor_only_boolean: Bitwise XOR is defined on words.

items.prompt: A one-hot mask for bit 31 is built by shifting a narrow signed literal whose usable positive range ends before that position. What is the safe diagnosis?

options.text: Construct the one in an explicitly wide or unsigned target representation before shifting.
Literal width and signedness never affect a mask.
Shift a smaller mask twice without changing the representation.
Replace the mask with 1 + 31.

feedback.reason: The mask constructor itself must have a representation capable of holding the selected bit.
feedback.details: Review should inspect the operand type before the shift, not only the destination variable.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.ignore_type: The shift can overflow or enter model-dependent territory.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.shift_twice: The final position still requires adequate width.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.use_addition: That is not one-hot at position 31.

items.prompt: A proof uses x AND -x on a signed negative value but never states width or overflow semantics. What should be requested?

options.text: An explicit fixed-width two's-complement model or an unsigned reinterpretation of the bit pattern.
A sorted list of x values.
A guarantee that the signed numeric result is positive.
A recursion-depth bound.

feedback.reason: The lowbit identity is a finite-bit-pattern statement.
feedback.details: Width and negation rules must be fixed, especially when the isolated bit is the sign position.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.positive_result: A one-hot sign bit may have a negative signed interpretation.

items.prompt: Why is the statement "masks are only performance optimizations" incorrect?

options.text: A mask can be the canonical semantic representation of a set of boolean features or resources.
Masks are always slower than collections.
Masks use no memory.
Masks are ordinary decimal arithmetic values.

feedback.reason: Bitmasks can encode domain state directly, not merely speed up an equivalent structure.
feedback.details: The bit-position mapping, width, and transitions form part of the data contract.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.only_arithmetic: Their positional membership meaning is central.

items.prompt: A complexity analysis labels every bitwise operation O(1), even though integers may contain arbitrarily many bits. What is the defect?

options.text: It applies a fixed-machine-word cost model to arbitrary-precision input.
Bitwise operations are never O(1).
Arbitrary-precision integers have no width.
The error affects space but never time.

feedback.reason: Complexity depends on whether width is a constant or part of the input.
feedback.details: An operation over w stored bits can cost O(w).

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.bitwise_never_constant: They are O(1) on a fixed-width word model.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.integers_no_width: They have a represented bit length that may grow.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.space_only: Processing wider values can increase both.

items.prompt: A subset-mask implementation uses one 32-bit word for n = 40 elements. What is the core defect?

options.text: The representation has fewer usable membership bits than elements.
The only issue is that 2^40 subsets are numerous.
The elements must be sorted first.
The code should use XOR instead of masks.

feedback.reason: One-bit-per-element encoding requires at least n usable positions.
feedback.details: A wider, arbitrary-precision, or multiword representation is necessary before considering enumeration cost.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.too_many_subsets_only: Enumeration is large, but the one-word encoding is already invalid.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.values_unsorted: Sorting does not create more bit positions.

items.prompt: Which are review red flags for bit-manipulation code? Select all that apply.

options.text: Complement, truncation, or negative representation is used without a declared width.
Right-shift reasoning does not state signedness or fill behavior.
Shift counts can reach or exceed the word width without a target-specific contract.
A one-hot mapping and valid range are explicitly documented.

feedback.reason: Bit code becomes fragile when representation assumptions remain implicit.
feedback.details: Documented width, signedness, mapping, and shift domain make reasoning auditable.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.one_hot_documented: That is a positive design property.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.missing_width: Those semantics depend on the word boundary.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.missing_signedness: Logical and arithmetic shifts may differ.

items.prompt: Which assumptions make fixed-width bit reasoning reviewable? Select all that apply.

options.text: The exact word width is stated.
Signed or unsigned interpretation is stated.
Logical or arithmetic right shift is stated.
The variable names encode the intended behavior.

feedback.reason: Representation assumptions should be explicit inputs to the proof.
feedback.details: Code style cannot replace a bit model.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.variable_names: Names are not a semantic contract.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.exact_width: It defines valid positions and truncation.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.signed_model: Numeric meaning and right shift can differ.

items.prompt: Which statements are false heuristics? Select all that apply.

options.text: Right shift always equals signed division by a power of two.
XOR is general arithmetic addition.
Masks are only performance optimizations.
Equal fixed-width patterns cancel under XOR.

feedback.reason: These heuristics erase the conditions that make bit techniques correct.
feedback.details: The pair-cancellation identity is unconditional for equal fixed-width patterns.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.xor_pairs: This is a valid identity, not a false heuristic.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.shift_division_all_signed: Negative rounding and fill rules can differ.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.masks_perf_only: They can be canonical semantic state.

items.prompt: Which edge cases should appear in a cross-cutting bit-manipulation review? Select all that apply.

options.text: Zero.
Only the most significant bit set.
Every bit set.
Only one ordinary small positive value.

feedback.reason: Boundary patterns reveal assumptions that ordinary low positive values hide.
feedback.details: A robust review also includes maximum shift positions and width-capacity limits.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.only_middle_positive: This misses width and sign boundaries.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.zero: It has no set bit and is the identity for several operations.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.sign_bit_only: It stresses signed interpretation and lowbit behavior.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.all_ones: It stresses -1, complement, and truncation assumptions.

items.prompt: Which defects can produce a correct-looking result on small positive values but fail at representation boundaries? Select all that apply.

options.text: Building a high-position mask in an inadequately narrow signed type.
Assuming logical and arithmetic right shifts are identical.
Ignoring high-bit loss during fixed-width left shift.
Using XOR commutativity to reorder operands.

feedback.reason: Representation-boundary bugs often remain invisible on low nonnegative examples.
feedback.details: The review set must deliberately cross sign, width, and overflow boundaries.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.xor_commutative: This identity remains valid at all fixed-width patterns.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.narrow_mask_literal: Low positions may work until the sign or width boundary.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.logical_vs_arithmetic: They match on nonnegative patterns but differ on negatives.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.missing_truncation: Small shifts may not overflow, while boundary cases do.

items.prompt: Arrange the debugging process for a suspicious bit-manipulation expression.

elements.text: State word width, signedness, and operation semantics.
Trace the exact input and intermediate bit patterns.
Test zero, sign-position, all-ones, and maximum valid shift cases.
Verify the final pattern and numeric interpretation against the required postcondition.

feedback.reason: Debugging begins by fixing the representation model before interpreting arithmetic meaning.
feedback.details: Boundary patterns then expose assumptions hidden by ordinary examples.

items.prompt: A routine processes q requests, each performing a constant number of operations on one fixed-width machine word. Select total time and auxiliary space.
constraints: The word width is a machine constant.

feedback.reason: The fixed-word model makes each request constant-time, not the entire sequence.
feedback.details: Processing q requests still requires linear total work.

items.prompt: A routine XOR-reduces n arbitrary-precision integers of at most w bits each. Select total time and accumulator space.
constraints: XOR of two w-bit represented integers costs O(w).

feedback.reason: The word-RAM O(1) assumption is invalid when operand width grows with input.
feedback.details: Both operation cost and stored accumulator scale with represented bit length.
